checks.a_analysis: The output content accurately reflects the headings and table data present in the source PDF. No paragraphs or sentences have been fabricated. The repeating heading 'NC OneMap Implementation: Initial Data Layers to Serve' correctly identifies the document title as it appears on both pages of the source.
The source document contains no contact information (phone numbers, emails, addresses, or URLs), and the output correctly reflects this absence without any hallucinations.

All sections and both pages from the source PDF are represented in the output. The table rows from page 1 and page 2 are all accounted for, including the continuation of the 'Wetlands' description across the page break.

The row counts in the output tables match the source document. Page 1 of the source has a table with 29 rows (including headers and sub-headers), which are all present in the first table of the JSON. Page 2 of the source has the continuation of the Wetlands row followed by 10 new data rows; these 11 rows are all present in the second table of the JSON.

checks.check_name: Significant text omission within a section
checks.a_analysis: The text within individual table cells has been faithfully transcribed. Descriptions such as those for 'Flood Zones', 'Soils', 'Hazardous Disposal Sites', and 'Demographics' contain all specific details (years, percentages, agency names) found in the original document.

checks.check_name: Minor text differences beyond acceptable reformatting
checks.a_analysis: No wording changes were identified that alter the meaning of the document. The transcription maintains the integrity of the data layer names and descriptions. The split of the 'Wetlands' description across two tables due to the page break is an acceptable reformatting choice that preserves the information.

checks.a_analysis: The document title 'NC OneMap Implementation: Initial Data Layers to Serve' is present at the beginning of the content for both page 1 and page 2 in the JSON output. However, in the source PDF, this title only appears once at the top of the first page.
checks.b_evidence_source: NC OneMap Implementation: Initial Data Layers to Serve
checks.b_evidence_output: "text": "NC OneMap Implementation: Initial Data Layers to Serve"

checks.a_analysis: The document uses H1 tags for the repeated title. While the title is duplicated (Check S1), the hierarchy itself does not skip levels or nest incorrectly based on the provided JSON structure.

The table is incorrectly split into two separate table objects in the JSON. Specifically, the 'Wetlands' description cell is truncated in the first table ('Wetlands areas from the US Fish and'). The remainder of the text from that same cell ('Wildlife Service, National Wetlands Inventory'), which appears at the top of page 2 in the source, is incorrectly identified as a header row in the second table in the JSON. Furthermore, the interior header row 'Other Important Layers | Description' on page 1 is incorrectly identified as a data row.
checks.b_evidence_source: The row for 'Wetlands' continues across the page break. Page 1 ends with 'Wetlands areas from the US Fish and' and page 2 begins with 'Wildlife Service, National Wetlands Inventory'.
checks.b_evidence_output: The first table ends with "text": "Wetlands areas from the US Fish and". The second table starts with a header cell: "content": [ { "text": "Wildlife Service, National Wetlands Inventory" ... } ]

checks.a_analysis: The content generally follows the top-to-bottom reading order of the source document, despite the structural errors in table identification.
The source document does not contain formal bulleted or numbered lists; the content is organized within a table structure.
There are no links present in the source document to be evaluated.

checks.a_analysis: The HTML document faithfully replicates the multi-table layout of the source PDF. The split across two pages in the source is represented by two separate table blocks in the HTML, which maintains the visual organization and flow of information. The placement of headings and content matches the source's intent.

There is a visual rendering error where the table from PDF Page 1 continues onto Page 2. In the source PDF, the 'Wetlands' description starts on page 1 and finishes on page 2. In the converted HTML, the second table block incorrectly places the tail end of the description ('Wildlife Service, National Wetlands Inventory') into the first column (Layer name) rather than the second column (Description). Additionally, the second table block lacks headers, though the source PDF also lacks repeated headers on the second page. The primary defect is the visual misalignment of the split row content.
checks.b_evidence_source: Page 1 ends with 'Wetlands' | 'Wetlands areas from the US Fish and'. Page 2 begins with description text: 'Wildlife Service, National Wetlands Inventory'.
checks.b_evidence_output: The first row of the second table block shows 'Wildlife Service, National Wetlands Inventory' in the left-hand column, which is reserved for layer names.

checks.a_analysis: The source document contains no meaningful images (photos, charts, logos). It is entirely text and table-based. No images were expected or missing from the output.

The main heading 'NC OneMap Implementation: Initial Data Layers to Serve' appears twice in the HTML document. However, this is a faithful representation of the source PDF, which includes this title at the top of both Page 1 and Page 2. Therefore, this is not a conversion defect.
